slides.title: Putting It Together: Risk, Controls, Defense in Depth
slides.subtitle: Cyber Foundations: a synthesis day that connects managing risk (2.1.E), choosing security controls (2.1.F), and layering them in defense in depth (2.1.G) into one end-to-end picture
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Lesson 2.1, Day 8 of 8 — the last day, and a synthesis day. No new essential knowledge today; instead we connect the last three days into one chain. Day 5 was managing risk, learning objective 2.1.E: the four strategies — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept. Day 6 was security controls, 2.1.F: classifying every control by type and by function. Day 7 was defense in depth, 2.1.G: why layering controls is effective. Today you'll see how they link: you assess a risk, you choose a strategy to manage it, and when that strategy is mitigation you reach for controls — layered in depth. We'll walk a worked comparison, trace one cumulative scenario from risk to resilient defense, run several practice prompts, and recap the vocabulary. By the end you can move across all three objectives in a single answer.

slides.prompt: Three days, three big ideas: the four ways to manage a risk (2.1.E), the ways to classify a security control (2.1.F), and why layering controls works (2.1.G). They are not separate topics — they're one process.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences from memory: when an organization decides to MITIGATE a risk, how do the other two topics — security controls and defense in depth — come into play? Connect all three ideas in your own words.
slides.directions: Independent writing. Be ready to share your thinking.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own, from memory — no notes yet. Three days, three ideas: managing risk in 2.1.E, classifying controls in 2.1.F, layering them in 2.1.G. In three to five sentences, connect them: when an organization chooses to mitigate a risk, how do controls and defense in depth come into the picture? You're building the bridge we'll spend today reinforcing. Mitigation, EK 2.1.E.4, is literally 'implement security controls' — and the smart way to implement them is in layers. That's the whole arc.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 8 Guided Notes have two review sections that tie the last three days together. Both are CB-required.

sections.title: The Chain: Risk → Strategy → Controls → Layers
sections.description: How managing risk (2.1.E), choosing controls (2.1.F), and layering them (2.1.G) form one connected process.

sections.title: Cumulative Scenario and Practice
sections.description: Work one scenario end-to-end and drill mixed prompts that cross all three objectives (LO 2.1.E, F, G).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.1 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Two review sections today, both CB-required because they consolidate required content. First, the chain: how managing risk, choosing controls, and layering them in defense in depth connect into one process — risk, then strategy, then controls, then layers. Second, a cumulative scenario worked end-to-end plus mixed practice that crosses all three objectives. Keep the packet open; this is the day everything clicks together, and Guided Notes are collected at the end.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Name the four risk-management strategies and identify which one is in play in a scenario, defining the residual risk that gets accepted (LO 2.1.E)
Classify any security control both ways at once — by type and by function — citing the EK for each (LO 2.1.F)
Explain why defense in depth is effective with both reasons — coverage and resilience (LO 2.1.G)
Chain all three objectives in one cumulative answer: from choosing to mitigate a risk to layering the controls that defend it (LO 2.1.E, LO 2.1.F, LO 2.1.G)

slides.script: Three objectives today, because we're reviewing all three — plus the skill that ties them together. Learning objective 2.1.E: name the four strategies for managing risk and spot which one is in play, defining the residual risk that gets accepted. Learning objective 2.1.F: classify any control both ways at once, by type and by function, citing the EK for each. Learning objective 2.1.G: explain why defense in depth is effective with both reasons — coverage and resilience. And the exam skill that chains them: move from choosing to mitigate a risk all the way to layering the controls that defend it. These three are the back half of Lesson 2.1, and the exam loves to chain them in one scenario, so by the end of today you can cross all three without losing the thread.

slides.label: Synthesis and Review
slides.script: Section eight, the final section of the lesson. Learning objectives 2.1.E, F, and G together. Today we connect, not add — every idea is one you've already met. As we go, watch for the seams where one objective hands off to the next. Twelve minutes here.

slides.heading: The Whole Arc on One Table
slides.subheading: From a risk to a layered defense — how 2.1.E, 2.1.F, and 2.1.G connect. Read it left to right.
rows: How likely is an attack on this asset, and how severe the damage?
Avoid, transfer, mitigate, or accept the risk
Classify each control by type and by function
Stack multiple control types for coverage and resilience
Name and accept the residual risk that remains
slides.script: Here is the entire back half of Lesson 2.1 on one table — read it top to bottom as a process. Step one, from the prior days, assess the risk: how likely is an attack on this asset and how severe the damage, EK 2.1.D.3. Step two, 2.1.E, choose a strategy: avoid, transfer, mitigate, or accept, EK 2.1.E.1. Step three, 2.1.F — and this only happens if you chose to mitigate: pick the controls and classify each by type and by function, EK 2.1.F.2 and F.3. Step four, 2.1.G: don't pick one control, layer multiple types for coverage and resilience, EK 2.1.G.1. Step five, back to 2.1.E: whatever risk remains after all that, name it and accept it — residual risk, EK 2.1.E.5. The highlighted column is the objective each step belongs to. That hand-off from E to F to G is exactly what a cumulative exam question tests. Copy this table — it's your map.

slides.heading: Cumulative Scenario: The School Online Store, End to End
slides.subheading: One scenario carried across all three objectives — from the risk decision to the layered defense.

left.title: Manage the Risk (LO 2.1.E)
left.definition: Choose strategies for the checkout vulnerability
examples: The store is core to fundraising, so AVOIDANCE isn't possible (EK 2.1.E.2)
The school MITIGATES the flaw by adding controls (EK 2.1.E.4)
It TRANSFERS remaining exposure with cyber-insurance (EK 2.1.E.3)
It ACCEPTS the small residual risk that's left (EK 2.1.E.5)

right.title: Choose and Layer Controls (LO 2.1.F, 2.1.G)
examples: Technical + preventative: encryption on card data (EK 2.1.F.2, F.3)
Technical + detective: a SIEM watching for odd checkout activity (EK 2.1.F.3)
Managerial: a password policy and access reviews for the admin account (EK 2.1.F.2)
Layered across data, application, and human — so one bypass isn't fatal (EK 2.1.G.3)

slides.footer: KEY SKILL: Watch the hand-off — choosing to MITIGATE (E.4) is the trigger to pick CONTROLS (F.2/F.3) and LAYER them (G.1–G.3). The same scenario exercises all three objectives at once.
slides.script: Let's carry one scenario across all three objectives — the school's online spirit-wear store with the checkout vulnerability. Left column, managing the risk, LO 2.1.E: the store funds activities, so avoidance isn't an option — EK 2.1.E.2 says you can't avoid a mission-critical activity. So the school mitigates the flaw with controls, E.4; transfers the leftover exposure by buying cyber-insurance, E.3; and consciously accepts the small residual risk that remains, E.5. Three of the four strategies in one decision. Right column, the controls themselves, LO 2.1.F and G: encryption on the card data is technical and preventative; a SIEM watching checkout activity is technical and detective; a password policy and access reviews for the admin account are managerial. And they're layered across the data, application, and human layers — so if one is bypassed, another still protects, EK 2.1.G.3. Watch the hand-off: choosing to mitigate is what triggers picking and layering controls. One scenario, all three objectives.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Cumulative Practice
slides.subheading: Mixed prompts across LO 2.1.E, F, and G. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A hospital cannot stop storing patient records but installs encryption, buys breach insurance, and documents the exposure it will live with. Name all THREE risk-management strategies used, with numbers, and define the residual risk it accepts (LO 2.1.E).
Classify each control BOTH ways — by type and by function: (a) a firewall, (b) a security camera, (c) an incident response plan, (d) vulnerability patching. (LO 2.1.F).

An adversary phishes an employee's password and gets past security-awareness training. Explain how a defense-in-depth strategy could still protect the data, and give BOTH reasons defense in depth is effective with their numbers (LO 2.1.G).

Tie it together: a company decides to MITIGATE a risk to its customer database. Walk from that decision to a layered defense — name two differently-typed controls you'd add, classify each by function explains why layering beats a single control (LO 2.1.E, F, G).
slides.script: Four cumulative questions, ten minutes, independent, full sentences — this is your review workout. Question one is pure 2.1.E: the hospital uses mitigation, transference, and acceptance — name all three with EKs and define residual risk. Question two is pure 2.1.F: classify a firewall, a camera, an IRP, and patching both ways — type and function, citing F.2 and F.3. Question three is pure 2.1.G: the phished password — explain how another layer still protects the data and give both reasons, coverage and resilience, with EKs. Question four is the integration question: a company chooses to mitigate, so walk it all the way to a layered defense — name two differently-typed controls, classify each by function, and cite the EK that says layering beats one control, that's G.3. Be precise with every EK number.

slides.heading: Vocabulary Recap (LO 2.1.E, F, G)

terms.definition: Avoid (stop the activity), transfer (shift the burden), mitigate (add controls), and accept (live with the residual risk) — the only options after a risk is assessed (EK 2.1.E.1).
The risk that remains after avoidance, transference, and mitigation; the level an organization deliberately chooses to accept (EK 2.1.E.5).
Where a control lives — physical (the building), technical (the digital space), or managerial (rules and policies) (EK 2.1.F.2).
What a control does — preventative (stop the attack), detective (spot it), or corrective (fix and restore) (EK 2.1.F.3).
A layered defense using multiple types of security controls to protect sensitive data and systems (EK 2.1.G.1).
The two reasons defense in depth is effective — addressing different threats each with the best-suited control, and backing a bypassed control with another (EK 2.1.G.2).

slides.script: A six-term recap across all three objectives — the must-know list for the exam. From 2.1.E: the four strategies — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept — and residual risk, the leftover you accept, E.5. From 2.1.F: control type, where it lives — physical, technical, managerial — F.2, and control function, what it does — preventative, detective, corrective — F.3. From 2.1.G: defense in depth, multiple control types layered, G.1, and the two reasons it works — coverage and resilience, G.2 and G.3. If you can define these six cold with EK numbers, you own the back half of Lesson 2.1. Get them all into your notes.

slides.heading: End of Lesson 2.1
todayPoints: Connected the whole arc: assess a risk, choose a strategy, pick controls, layer them, accept what's left
Reviewed the four risk-management strategies and residual risk (LO 2.1.E)
Reviewed classifying controls by type and by function (LO 2.1.F)
Reviewed why defense in depth is effective — coverage and resilience (LO 2.1.G)
Worked one cumulative scenario end-to-end and drilled mixed practice across all three objectives
nextPoints: Lesson 2.1, Cyber Foundations, is complete — you can move from a risk to a resilient, layered defense
Carry the chain forward: managing risk and choosing controls underpins everything in Unit 2
Keep 'coverage and resilience' ready — it's the model answer whenever defense in depth appears
slides.teaser: You can now take any asset, weigh its risk, decide how to manage it, choose the right controls, and layer them so one failure isn't a catastrophe. That full chain is what the rest of Unit 2 builds on.
slides.script: Lesson 2.1 is complete. You can run the whole chain: assess a risk, choose how to manage it from the four strategies, and when you mitigate, pick the right controls — classified by type and function — and layer them in depth for coverage and resilience, then accept the residual risk that always remains. That end-to-end thinking is exactly what the exam rewards when it chains 2.1.E, F, and G in one scenario. Carry it forward — managing risk and choosing controls underpins the rest of Unit 2. Review your Guided Notes one more time: the four strategies, the two control classifications, and the two reasons defense in depth works. Well done on Lesson 2.1.
